schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott fix the legal ground: the daughter of Pharaoh is set apart, and the sin is multiplying wives (Deut. 17:17) and marrying forbidden nations.
Gill: the love was lustful, not lawful — foreign, unlawful, and many.
Matthew Henry laments the fall of 'the beauty of Israel,' tracing it to the flesh and the multiplying of wives against the royal law.
Clarke: the strange women were idolaters, and Pharaoh's daughter one of them; the claim she turned proselyte has no evidence.

debates.question: Is the daughter of Pharaoh included in the censure?

sides.position: she is distinguished from the blamed wives; no Egyptian idolatry is charged to Solomon
she too was a strange woman and an idolater, not proven a proselyte

schools.summary: Gill ties the prohibition to the Hittites among the seven nations; the danger was spiritual — intermarriage would end in apostasy.
Keil and Delitzsch: the 700 princesses and 300 concubines are round numbers gathered across the whole reign, far exceeding the court of Song 6:8.
Gill: the thousand is prodigious, but many were kept for state after eastern custom rather than use.
Ellicott: the harem was an adjunct of magnificence, the relations largely nominal; the numbers are excessive beside Song 6:8 and possibly corrupt.
Clarke takes the numbers at full weight: a thousand wives make piety impossible, and Solomon went as far in iniquity as possible.

debates.question: How should the numbers of the harem be taken?

sides.position: partly oriental display, and perhaps an ancient textual corruption
at full weight, as the measure of a great and deliberate apostasy

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: in old age the flesh mastered the spirit, and his heart was no longer 'shalem,' wholly the Lord's, unlike David who never fell to idolatry; Ellicott probes an eclectic, philosophic indifferentism.
Gill reckons him near sixty, finding his age an aggravation of the folly.

debates.question: What was the nature of Solomon's apostasy?

sides.position: a divided heart, no longer perfect toward the Lord as David's was
an eclectic tolerance, honouring all faiths as phases of one Supreme Power — more monstrous than open idolatry

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott identify Ashtoreth (moon-goddess of impurity) and Milcom/Molech (the fire-god of child sacrifice); the building of altars, even without personal sacrifice, is idolatry.
Gill: enticed by the Zidonian women, Solomon went after Astarte, the Venus and moon of the heathen.

debates.question: Did Solomon personally sacrifice to the idols, or only build their altars?

sides.position: Scripture does not say he sacrificed; the wives burnt incense, but building the altars was itself irreconcilable idolatry

schools.summary: Gill: idolatry is evil, than which nothing is more provoking to God.
Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the heading summing the acts — building high places for Chemosh, Molech, and all the wives' gods (the three altars of 2 Kings 23:13).
Ellicott: the high places stood on the Mount of Olives (the 'Mount of Offence'), facing and rivalling the Temple; Tophet lay below in the valley of Hinnom.
Gill (with Jarchi): the hill before Jerusalem is the Mount of Olives, later the 'mount of corruption.'
Clarke: the hill before Jerusalem was the Mount of Olives.
Gill: having granted a shrine to one wife, Solomon could not refuse the rest — the logic of compromise that ended in altars for all.
Ellicott stresses the twice-repeated appearing of the Lord (1 Kings 3:5; 9:2) as a peculiar privilege and responsibility, the hinge to the coming judgment.
Matthew Henry: the sin was base ingratitude toward the God who appeared twice, and wilful disobedience of an express command.
Keil and Delitzsch: the emphasis falls on God's appearing in person, not by prophets, to warn him — grace spurned draws down anger.
Gill: his heart turned from the fear of the Lord, without which he could not have given in to idolatry.
Clarke: vice is vice, but Solomon's light and privilege aggravate his guilt beyond the ignorant Sidonians'.
Gill: a further aggravation — the sin was against an express command, personally given, with warning and threat attached.
Keil and Delitzsch read it as the second half of the accusation: he kept not the covenant, and so the sentence answers to the covenant broken.
Gill: the word came not by vision now but by a prophet, likely Ahijah; the kingdom is given to his 'servant,' Jeroboam.
Clarke reads even the sentence as a warning of mercy — might Solomon yet have recovered himself and found grace in it?

debates.question: Was the sentence also an offer of mercy?

sides.position: the announcement of judgment was itself another warning Solomon might have heeded

schools.summary: Gill: the reprieve is 'for David's sake' — not for his merits but for the promises made to him (2 Sam. 7).
Matthew Henry: the stroke is deferred to the son, and it is a favour when judgments come not in our own days.
Clarke: the one tribe is kept because the line of Messiah, the Lion of Judah, must be preserved; Jerusalem, type of the true Church, kept for the true David.
Matthew Henry: Judah (with Benjamin) remains for Jerusalem's and the temple's sake; God did not quickly nor wholly take what Solomon did not quickly nor wholly forsake.
Gill: God will not rend away the whole kingdom.
Matthew Henry: sin made even a small enemy formidable — if God be against us, the very grasshopper is a burden; look through the instrument to the Author.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: Hadad was of Edom's royal seed, the last scion escaping as a child; 'Hadad' a royal title, like a Syriac name of the sun.
Gill: God chastised Solomon with the rod of men, raising adversaries in his old age.
Clarke: 'adversary' is a satan; where once there was no satan, now three rise at once — Hadad, Rezon, Jeroboam.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott set the war in its parallel records (2 Sam. 8; 1 Chron. 18; Ps. 60), a campaign of ruthless extermination of every male under Joab.
Gill: David subdued Edom and set garrisons in the land.
Clarke: Joab in six months exterminated all the males save Hadad; the Targum reads 'take the spoils of the slain.'
Gill: Joab remained with the whole army.
Keil and Delitzsch: six months, the whole host, every male cut off — and out of it the child Hadad fled through Midian and Paran to Pharaoh's endowed refuge.
Gill: Hadad fled while Joab was burying the slain.
Clarke: 'a little child' (naar katan) means a boy old enough to fear danger and escape, not an infant.
Ellicott traces the route (Midian, Paran) and the Egyptian politics — a Tanite Pharaoh, jealous of Israel, welcoming an Edomite claimant as later Jeroboam.
Gill: Midian lay in their way, and they made some stay there.
Clarke: from Midian to Paran they gathered a company, then went to Egypt, where Hadad became Pharaoh's favourite.
Keil and Delitzsch: Hadad married the sister of Tahpenes the queen, and his son Genubath was reared among Pharaoh's children; Ellicott notes Tahpenes is otherwise unknown.
Gill: the favour was owed perhaps to Hadad's comely person and princely qualities.
Gill: the name Genubath signifies 'stealth,' perhaps recalling Hadad's own stolen escape.
Ellicott: the weaning in Pharaoh's house, with its customary festival, marked the child's admittance into the royal family of Egypt.
Gill: the death of a king of David's fame would soon be known in Egypt.
Keil and Delitzsch: on hearing David and Joab were dead, Hadad asked to depart; Pharaoh's 'what is lacking?' is love urging him to stay.
Matthew Henry (with Peter Martyr): Hadad's homeward longing amid plenty pictures the believer's holy desire for heaven even when the world smiles most.
Keil and Delitzsch: Pharaoh's words are pure attachment; his consent must be supplied, as elsewhere in Scripture.
Gill: Pharaoh's question covers everything a prince might want — equipage, provision, honour.
Clarke: Hadad did not avow his real intention, since Israel and Egypt were then at peace.
Ellicott: 'Rezon' means 'prince'; he may be the Hezion later king of Damascus, within the Hadad-titled Syrian dynasty.
Gill: one adversary from the south (Hadad), now another from the north (Rezon).
Clarke reads Rezon as the fulfilment of Nathan's word — chastening with the rod of men (2 Sam. 7:14).
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: Rezon gathered fugitives when David smote Zobah, then seized Damascus and reigned, his independent power dating to Solomon's later days.
Gill: the band was gathered from the scattered forces of his former master.
Clarke: driven out by David, Rezon recovered Damascus after Solomon's defection and joined Hadad — some think the two the same man.
Ellicott flags the doubtful text of the abrupt clause 'beside the mischief that Hadad did,' where the LXX reads Edom for Syria.
Gill: 'all the days of Solomon' means from his first going into idolatry to the end, once his defence had departed.
Matthew Henry dwells on 'abhorred Israel' — even the best of peoples will be hated by some.
Matthew Henry: Jeroboam rose by industry — the diligent man stands before kings; Solomon promoted the industrious as David the faithful.
Ellicott: 'the great rebel against the house of David,' raised by Solomon's favour from an utterly dependent condition.
Gill: this Jeroboam of Ephraim is not Sheba the son of Bichri of Benjamin.
Clarke: at Millo, as Solomon grew tyrannical, the seeds of disaffection were sown, and the enterprising Jeroboam used the discontent.
Keil and Delitzsch: the 'lifting up of the hand' belongs to the time of the heavy works; Ellicott dates Millo some twenty years in, when building — and the people's burdens — grew.
Gill: the occasion was Jeroboam's advancement and what passed with Ahijah.
Ellicott: 'mighty man of valour' means strong and capable, not warlike; the levy once for the Temple now burdens Israel itself, and in Ahijah prophecy re-emerges from abeyance.
Gill: a man of great bodily strength and fortitude of mind.
Gill: Jeroboam went out either to enter his office or having settled his accounts with Solomon.
Clarke: Ahijah was a writer of Solomon's history (2 Chron. 9:29), perhaps the very prophet of the earlier message.

Ellicott: it was Ahijah's own new garment, torn like Samuel's mantle to symbolise the rending of the kingdom, in the tradition of prophetic sign-acts; Keil and Delitzsch carry the sign into its interpretation.
Gill weighs the ambiguous pronoun — the garment may be Jeroboam's or the prophet's own.

sides.position: Ahijah's own, like Samuel's rent mantle
possibly Jeroboam's, newly got to appear before the king (left open)

schools.summary: Clarke: the garment is the kingdom, the twelve pieces the twelve tribes, the ten given to Jeroboam the future northern kingdom in Samaria against Judah in Jerusalem.
Gill: ten of the twelve pieces are an emblem of the ten tribes.
Matthew Henry: two tribes, called one, stay sure to David that he may have a lamp (Ps. 132:17); David is not rejected as Saul, for the Messiah must arise from his house.
Gill: Judah and Benjamin are reckoned together as one tribe.
Matthew Henry: the kingdom is given to Jeroboam not for his desert but to chastise Solomon's apostasy — Israel deserved so bad a prince (Deut. 9:4).
Gill: 'forsaken me' means forsaken my worship — Solomon and the people who followed his example.
Keil and Delitzsch let the roll of Ashtoreth, Chemosh, and Milcom stand as the substance of the charge.
Matthew Henry: Solomon keeps the throne all his life, not for his own sake — he had forfeited it — but for David's; children may fare better for a parent's piety.
Gill: no part of the kingdom is taken from Solomon's own hand in his lifetime.
Keil and Delitzsch: the judgment is staged across two reigns — deferred through Solomon, executed on his son, ten tribes to Jeroboam.
Gill: all is taken from the son except Judah and Benjamin.
Matthew Henry: Jeroboam must wait with patience, not stretching his hand against the living king.
Clarke: 'a light alway' means David's line shall never fail — in reference to the Messiah, David's light and a light to enlighten the Gentiles (Luke 2:32).
Gill: the 'light' is a kingdom and an illustrious prince, continuing the pure worship at Jerusalem.
Matthew Henry: the lamp of David that God swore to keep burning (Ps. 132:17).
Clarke: 'all that thy soul desireth' shows Jeroboam had already affected the kingdom; God grants the ten tribes but bounds his ambition to spare David's line.
Gill: God will take Jeroboam from his low estate and make him king.
Clarke: God would have continued Jeroboam's posterity on the throne; the sure house was forfeited by his wickedness, not withheld.
Keil and Delitzsch: the conditional promise (obedience as David's) did not justify Jeroboam's rebellion.
Gill: the condition is to hearken to all God commands and be obedient.
Clarke: 'not for ever' — David's seed afflicted only until Messiah come to establish the throne for ever; even Jarchi looks for restoration to David's house.
Gill: the affliction is 'for this,' for the idolatry Solomon committed and connived at.
Matthew Henry: Solomon foolishly fights the counsel of God he had himself taught (Prov. 19:21), while Jeroboam waits in patient exile, sure of a kingdom.
Clarke and Ellicott anchor the flight in history: Shishak (Sheshenk of the monuments), founder of the Bubastite dynasty, welcoming the rebel as he later invades Judah (1 Kings 14:25).
Gill: it was folly to seek to defeat the counsel of God, which Solomon had been assured would stand.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch: the prophets served as annalists (Nathan, Ahijah, Iddo); Kings is a compilation from temple records and archives; whether Solomon repented cannot be ascertained.
Gill: the lost 'book of the acts' was written by Solomon or a chronicler or several prophets (2 Chron. 9:29).
Matthew Henry: an uninspired history now lost, from which the sacred writer took what God saw fit.
Clarke: the acts were written by Nathan, Ahijah, and Iddo, the originals long since perished.
Ellicott: forty years, matching David, is no mere round number; Josephus's eighty is a textual error; the Greek alternative life of Jeroboam is far inferior to the Hebrew.
Gill uses Rehoboam's age to refute the tradition that Solomon was twelve at his accession, and Josephus's ninety-four as too great.
Clarke (with Calmet): about eighteen at accession, dead near fifty-eight — as long a life as his excesses could expect.

debates.question: How long did Solomon live and reign?

sides.position: forty years' reign, an ordinary lifespan shortened by excess
Josephus's eighty years, rejected as error or confusion of the two reigns

schools.summary: Clarke reads the silent grave as condemnation: no proof of repentance, Ecclesiastes no penitent's language, Solomon died in his sins — against unconditional final perseverance.
Matthew Henry: his life, unlike his reign, was cut short by sin; the disappointed king's 'vanity' points past himself to one greater than Solomon.
Keil and Delitzsch: whether Solomon repented cannot be settled from Scripture, though Ecclesiastes, if his, shows a man who found all vanity and feared God.
Gill: he died as his fathers did.

debates.question: Did Solomon repent and die a saved man?

sides.position: there is no evidence; he died in his sins, a warning against presuming on repentance
there is reason to hope he repented, Ecclesiastes being his penitential sermon
it cannot be ascertained; Ecclesiastes shows at least a return to the fear of God

themes: let him that standeth take heed
